title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about theatre and the performing arts. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Living Stage
content.subtitle: An article about the enduring power of theatre in the modern world.
content.text:
At a time when screens of every size compete for attention, theatre occupies a peculiar position: an ancient art form that requires its audience to leave the house, sit in the dark, and watch human beings perform in the same physical space. This apparently old-fashioned arrangement has not only survived but continues to generate intense loyalty among those who experience it. [31] The reason, many practitioners argue, is that theatre offers something no recorded medium can replicate: the knowledge, shared by performer and audience alike, that anything could go wrong — and the aliveness that comes from that shared risk. [/31]

Theatre has always been a contested space. From its origins in ancient Greece, where it served both religious and civic functions, to the Elizabethan era when it was enjoyed by royalty and groundlings in the same yard, it has oscillated between being considered a morally dangerous entertainment and a vehicle for social commentary and truth-telling. [32] This tension has never entirely been resolved. Theatre still has the power to cause genuine controversy — and directors, writers, and actors still sometimes pay a price for the positions they take on stage. [/32]

The economics of theatre are notoriously precarious. Even in cities with strong theatre cultures, producing a new play involves considerable financial risk. The costs of rehearsal space, performance rights, set construction, lighting, and marketing are substantial, while ticket revenue rarely covers them without additional subsidy. [33] This dependence on public or private funding gives theatre a fragile relationship with the political and commercial entities on which it relies — a relationship that can subtly influence which stories get told and which voices are amplified. [/33]

Contemporary theatre has responded to the digital age with a mixture of resistance and adaptation. Some practitioners have embraced technology wholeheartedly, incorporating projection, live video feeds, and interactive digital elements into their productions. Others have moved in the opposite direction, stripping performance back to its essentials: a performer, an audience, and a shared space. [34] Both impulses reflect a search for what is irreducibly theatrical — what the form can do that no other art can do as well. [/34]

Diversity and representation have become central debates in theatrical communities worldwide. For much of its history, mainstream theatre reproduced, on stage and behind it, the power structures of the societies it claimed to reflect and challenge. [35] The past two decades have seen significant efforts to change this — to ensure that the stories performed, the people who perform them, and the audiences who watch them better reflect the full range of human experience. Progress has been real but uneven, and the conversation remains live. [/35]

What theatre does for those who make it — and for those who watch it — is difficult to quantify but easy to recognise. [36] At its best, a theatrical performance creates a moment of collective attention in which a room full of strangers agrees, for a few hours, to believe in something together. In a fragmented world, there is something quietly radical about that. [/36]

questions.question: According to the article, what gives live theatre a unique quality that recorded media cannot offer?
options: A) The elaborate sets and costumes that film cannot reproduce.
B) The opportunity to watch well-known actors perform in person.
C) The sense of shared risk between performers and the audience.
D) The historical tradition that gives theatre cultural authority.
questions.explanation: Theatre offers 'the knowledge, shared by performer and audience alike, that anything could go wrong — and the aliveness that comes from that shared risk'.

questions.question: The author's point about theatre causing 'genuine controversy' today suggests that
options: A) theatre should avoid politically sensitive topics to attract wider audiences.
B) its potential to challenge and provoke has remained over time.
C) modern audiences are more easily offended than those of previous generations.
D) governments are more likely to censor theatre than other art forms.
questions.explanation: The mention of ongoing controversy and consequences for artists who take positions on stage shows that theatre's challenging power 'has never entirely been resolved' — it persists today.

questions.question: The author implies that theatre's reliance on funding may
options: A) prevent smaller theatre companies from producing original work.
B) influence which types of stories and voices are given a platform.
C) make theatre less accessible to audiences from disadvantaged backgrounds.
D) lead theatres to prioritise commercial productions over new writing.
questions.explanation: The dependence on funders can 'subtly influence which stories get told and which voices are amplified', suggesting editorial influence over content.

questions.question: The two contrasting responses to digital technology described in the article both reflect
options: A) a disagreement about how large theatre audiences should be.
B) a desire to define what makes theatre distinctive as an art form.
C) an attempt to attract younger audiences who prefer digital media.
D) the influence of commercial theatre on experimental productions.
questions.explanation: Both the embrace of technology and the return to essentials are described as 'a search for what is irreducibly theatrical — what the form can do that no other art can do as well'.

questions.question: What does the author say about efforts to improve diversity in theatre?
options: A) They have been largely successful in transforming the theatre industry.
B) They have focused mainly on increasing the diversity of audiences.
C) They have made genuine but incomplete advances over the past twenty years.
D) They have been resisted by most mainstream theatre companies.
questions.explanation: The author states 'Progress has been real but uneven', indicating genuine but incomplete advances.

questions.question: The author describes a successful theatrical performance as 'quietly radical' because
options: A) it typically deals with politically radical subject matter.
B) it brings strangers together in shared belief, which is rare in modern life.
C) it challenges audience members to reconsider their political views.
D) it happens in a space separate from the commercial entertainment industry.
questions.explanation: The performance is described as creating 'collective attention' where 'strangers agree to believe in something together', which is considered 'quietly radical' in 'a fragmented world'.
